intent:
PREMISE CONTRACT (story 30)
  one_sentence_event:    The narrator opens an old box at home, finds a photograph of a face she cannot at first name, looks at it again until she remembers the face is her childhood friend; she then chooses to write that friend a letter rather than store the photograph away.
  change_of_state:       at s0 the photograph sits forgotten inside a closed box, the friend's name lost from the narrator's memory; at the closer the friend's name is recovered and the narrator is writing TO her — the past has become a present-tense correspondence, not a kept artifact.
  scene_novelty_claim:   I am NOT writing a classroom / park / station story — this is a private evening at home. Crucially, I am also NOT writing a 'parent reveals narrator's forgotten past' story (the shape stories 28 and 29 both used). The narrator remembers on her own; no second character explains anything to her.
  anchor_novelty_claim:  Anchor is 写真 (photograph). 写真 has never been an anchor before; it has never appeared in the corpus (mints W00154).
  closer_shape_claim:    Closer shape: outward action — narrator picks up paper and writes a letter. NOT a noun-pile, NOT a 「Nは Adj です」 mirror; not story 27's 「Xは『Z』と言いました」 dialogue closer; not story 28's 「Nは静かにVました」 single-mover-with-adverb shape; not story 29's 「NをNに入れて、Nを出ました」 pencil-into-box-leave-room shape (story 30 closer is letter-being-written, no 入れる, no leaving).
  why_not_filler:        narrator (must — sole actor; the discovery is inside her head); box (must — R1 carry; the container that surfaced the photograph); photograph (must — anchor, the trigger of the entire memory chain); face (must — the specific element of the photograph that resists then yields recognition); 思い出す (must — the verb of the recovery itself); friend (must — the photograph's referent and the addressee of the closing letter); letter+paper (must — the present-tense action that turns memory into outward gesture).
  obligations_absorbed:  must-reinforce W00151 箱 (s0 — frame), W00150 昔 (s5 — narrator's recall + s6 — naming the friend's era), W00152 なぜ (s3 — narrator's interior question), W00153 覚える (s2 — narrator's failure to remember the name). Must-introduce N5_hoshii via 「友達からの手紙がほしい」 in s7 — the desire is what motivates the closer's action, not a bolt-on. Mother is INTENTIONALLY absent: stories 28 and 29 both used a parent/teacher to reveal the narrator's past; story 30 must not be the third in that pattern (literary reviewer flagged this trap).

FORBIDDEN THIS STORY:
  scene_class    (last 3 stories): classroom, park, station — chose home (kept clear).
  anchor_object  (last 5 stories): ノート, 傘, 手紙, 絵, 鉛筆 — chose 写真 (kept clear).
  closer_shape   (last 3 stories): noun-WA-noun-NI-noun, noun-WA-noun-noun-TO-noun, noun-WO-noun-NI-noun-noun-WO-noun — closer is two-clause action ending on 書きました (an outward letter-writing); does not mirror those shapes.
  opening_seqs   (last 3 stories): 友達/二人/公園, 夕方/駅/母, 朝/教室/先生 — opening tokens are 部屋/古い/箱 (kept clear).

New grammar: N5_hoshii — 〜がほしい (want a thing), introduced via the narrator's interior thought 「友達からの手紙がほしい」 in s7. The request is internal (no second character to address), which both fits the solitary scene and avoids the audience-direction defect 〜てください sometimes shows. Mints are 写真, 顔, こと, 思い出す (4, target = 4).

sentences.jp: 部屋で古い箱を開けました。
sentences.en: In my room, I opened an old box.

sentences.jp: 箱に古い写真がありました。
sentences.en: Inside the box there was an old photograph.

sentences.jp: 写真の人の顔を見ましたが、名前を覚えていませんでした。
sentences.en: I looked at the face of the person in the photograph, but I did not remember the name.

sentences.jp: 「なぜこの写真をまだ持っているのか」と思いました。
sentences.en: "Why am I still keeping this photograph?" I thought.

sentences.jp: ゆっくり、写真を見ました。
sentences.en: Slowly, I looked at the photograph.

sentences.jp: その人の名前を思い出しました。
写真の人は、昔の友達でした。
sentences.en: The person in the photograph was a friend from long ago.

sentences.jp: 友達からの手紙がほしいと思いました。
sentences.en: I wanted a letter from her, I thought.

sentences.jp: 紙を出して、手紙を書きました。
sentences.en: I took out some paper and wrote her a letter.
